english_query: strict conditional, defeasible — agricultural
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a rule that says when a farm uses a certain way of growing food, then the farm will get a good result. They say this rule is strict, meaning that normally it must hold every time, unless there is a clear reason that makes it not work. The second idea they add is that the rule can be set aside when another situation appears, for example when the weather is very different or when the soil is not like usual. This makes the rule defeasible, that is, it can be broken when there is a good reason.

From these two ideas they think that we should not always follow the first rule without looking at the other situation. They say that because the rule can be set aside, we are allowed to choose a different way of growing food when the special situation happens. In other words, they think the rule does not force us to act the same way in every case, and that we can change what we do when the special case is present.

The thinking behind this is generally good. It is sensible to have a rule that works most of the time but also to allow an exception when something else matters. Saying that the rule can be set aside only when we have a clear reason helps keep the rule useful while still giving room for change. The only weak point would be if the reason for setting the rule aside is not clear enough, because then people might ignore the rule too often. Overall, the step from the first idea to the second idea follows a reasonable line of thought.

english_query: why following rules is not enough if you do not care about people
ugf_response: First, we notice that many groups give each of us a list of things that are said to be the right way to act. Then, we see that if a person only does those things because they are on the list, the person can still act in a way that hurts other people or does not help them. This happens because the list does not tell us how we should feel about the other people or how we should try to make them feel good. Next, we think about a time when someone helps another person even though the list does not ask for it, and we see that the feeling of care for the other person can guide us to do more than just the list. Then, we see that if we only look at the list and ignore the care we have for other people, we can end up doing things that are on the list but still make others sad. This means that the list by itself is not enough. Finally, we understand that to act in a way that is really good for everyone, we need both the list and a real care for the other people, because the care tells us when the list is missing something important.

english_query: why treating people as things to use is always wrong
ugf_response:
The first idea says that a person is more than a thing that can be moved or changed for another’s need. It says that a person has thoughts, feelings, wants and a sense of self, and that these things make a person different from a plain object that can be taken or used without any thought of the person inside. The claim is that this description of a person is true for every person, no matter where they live or what they do.

The second idea says that because a person is not just a plain thing, it must be wrong to treat a person as if they were a plain thing that can be used. It says that any action that makes a person into a tool for another’s plan, or that treats the person’s value only as a way to get something, goes against the fact that the person has thoughts and feelings. From this it follows, in the view being explained, that every time someone tries to use another person for their own end, that act is a bad act.

The thinking that links the first idea to the second idea is mostly strong. If it is true that a person has thoughts and feelings, then it does make sense to say that hurting those thoughts or feelings by treating the person as a plain tool is not right. The step from “people have feelings” to “using them is always bad” is a good step when the use ignores the person’s own wishes and causes hurt. However, the thinking can be too strict if it does not see that sometimes people work together and help each other in ways that look like use but are done with the agreement and good will of all involved. In those cases the act is not a hurtful use but a shared effort. So the core of the thinking is sound, but the claim that it is always wrong may be a little too broad because it does not allow for situations where people agree to help each other in ways that respect each person’s thoughts and feelings.

english_query: probabilistic conditional, affirming_consequent — social_causation
ugf_response: First, the person says that when one thing often happens together with another thing, we can see that the two things are linked in the real world. They point to many times where the first thing and the second thing appear at the same time and say that this is a true observation about how people act. Second, from that observation they think that if we see the second thing, we can say the first thing must have happened. They take the pattern “if the first thing happens, then the second thing happens” and turn it around, saying that the second thing shows the first thing. They also think that because the first thing can make the second thing happen for a group of people, the first thing must be the cause of the second thing for the whole group. Third, the thinking does not hold up well. Seeing the second thing does not prove the first thing, especially when the link is only a chance and not a certain rule. Many other things can also lead to the second thing, and the first thing might not have happened at all. So the step from “the first thing often goes with the second thing” to “the second thing means the first thing happened” is a bad step in reasoning. The conclusion that the first thing causes the second thing for all people is therefore not a good conclusion.

english_query: the way to help yourself think about hard questions step by step
ugf_response:
We start with a person who wants to think about a hard ask that is not easy to answer. The person feels that the ask is big and may make the mind feel heavy, so the person looks for a way to make the thinking easier. The idea is to break the big ask into many small parts and to look at each part one at a time, like walking a path that has many little stones instead of one big rock.

The hard ask itself can be something like “What should I do when I feel scared of a change?” or “How can I know if my work is good?” The person may feel stuck because the ask has many pieces that seem to push against each other. By taking a small piece, writing it down, and then looking at what comes next, the mind can see a clear line instead of a tangled knot. The person can also pause, breathe, and ask a simple version of the ask, such as “What is the first thing I can do right now?” and then move on to the next simple ask.

Different people may give different replies about how to help the mind in this situation. One reply may say that writing the small pieces on paper helps the mind see them as separate and not as a huge wall. Another reply may say that talking with a friend or a trusted person lets the mind hear new words and can make the big ask feel less scary. A third reply may say that taking a short walk or doing a simple move can let the mind rest, and when it comes back it can see the small pieces more clearly. Some people think that using a timer, such as working for five minutes on one piece and then checking the work, keeps the mind from drifting. Others believe that looking at the big ask as a story, where each small piece is a part of the story, helps the mind keep a sense of why the ask matters. All of these replies work because they each give the mind a way to turn a big, heavy ask into a set of little, lighter asks that can be handled one after another.

english_query: counterexample to: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authori
ugf_response: The first idea says that a way that people share power is right only when that way gets its power from the people who live under it, and that this condition is needed for the way to be right. The second idea says that there is a case that does not fit this rule, a case where a way gets its power from the people but still seems not right, or where a way seems right even though it does not get its power from the people. This case is offered as a way to show that the first idea does not hold for every situation. The third idea looks at whether this move from the case to the claim is a good move. It asks if the case really shows the rule is false, or if perhaps the case misses some other part of the rule or uses a different meaning of “gets its power from the people.” If the case does not match the exact meaning of the rule, then the thinking is not strong; if the case does match and still breaks the rule, then the thinking is strong and the first idea would need to be changed. In short, the first idea states a condition for rightness, the second idea brings a case that seems to break that condition, and the third idea judges whether the jump from the case to the claim is a solid step or a weak one.

english_query: why being sure about something does not make it right
ugf_response: First, we notice that a person can feel sure about a thought. Then we see that that feeling of sure does not tell us if the thought matches the real thing that is out there. This means that a mind can be sure even when the real thing is different. The reason is that the mind uses ideas that may be wrong, and the feeling of sure does not check those ideas against what we can see. We also see that other people can look at the same thing, be less sure, and still be closer to the real thing. So we learn that we need to look, watch, and think about what we can see, not just trust the feeling of being sure. This shows why being sure about something does not make it right.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circ
ugf_response:
The first idea says that a statement that is always true is the same as a statement that is true in every situation. In other words, the two things happen together: if a statement never fails, then it must be true no matter what, and if it is true no matter what, then it never fails.

The second idea is that someone gives a different case and says this case shows the first idea is not right. They pick a statement that they think is true in every situation but that they also think is not an always‑true statement, and they say this makes the first idea false.

When we look at the second idea we see that the thinking is not good. A statement that is true in every situation is, by definition, an always‑true statement, so the example does not actually break the first idea. The problem is that the example mixes up the meaning of “always true” with something else, and because of that the step from the example to the claim that the first idea is wrong does not hold. Thus the first idea stays correct and the reasoning that tries to knock it down is flawed.

english_query: probabilistic conditional, hypothetical_syllogism — games_and_sport
ugf_response:
The first idea says that when a player has a good chance to win a game or sport, then the player will feel more sure about the game and will try harder. This idea can be true in many cases because people often feel more sure when they think they can win, and they often work harder when they feel sure. It is not true for every person, however, because some people may feel nervous or may not change how they work even when they think they can win.

The second idea says that when a player tries harder, then the player will become better at the game or sport. This idea is generally true. When a person puts more effort into practice or play, they usually learn more and get better at the skill.

The third idea is what the person thinks follows from the first two ideas: if a player has a good chance to win a game or sport, then the player will become better at that game or sport. This follows the form of a chain of “if‑then” thoughts, so the way of thinking is correct in shape. The thinking is good in that the conclusion does follow from the two earlier thoughts if those thoughts are true. Because the first idea is not always true for every player, the whole chain is not always true for every player, so the reasoning is not completely sound even though the logical pattern is right. In short, the pattern of the thought is good, but the truth of the first part makes the final claim only sometimes right.

english_query: what makes someone happy in the deepest way, not just for a moment
ugf_response: First, we see that a person can feel good when they do something that fits with who they are and when they feel that they belong with other people. Then we see that this feeling can stay for a long time if the thing they do also helps them grow or learn. A feeling that stays for a long time is not just a short happy feeling but a still feel that the world is good for them. Then we see that this still feel often comes from three parts: having a want, having good care with other people, and having time to use what they know. Having a want means they see a way to move to something they think is right for them. Having good care with other people means they feel that they are not alone and that they can share. Having time to use what they know means they can act in a way that matches what they have learned and feel good. When all three are there, the person can feel a very strong, long happy feeling that stays even when the quick happy feeling goes away.

english_query: counterexample to: A judgement is synthetic a priori if and only if (i) the predicate is not contai
ugf_response:
The first idea that the writer puts forward is that there is a case that shows a certain rule about a kind of statement does not work. The rule they talk about says that a statement that we can know before we see any thing and that is not just true by the meaning of its words must have a part that says something about the thing, and that part must not already be held inside the thing itself. The writer says the case they give has a statement that fits the first part of the rule – it is a statement we can know before we see any thing and it is not just true by the meaning of its words – but the part that says something about the thing does in fact already hold inside the thing. In other words, the writer claims the rule says one thing and the case shows something different.

The second idea that follows from the first is that because this case exists, the rule cannot be right. The writer thinks that one example that does not fit the rule is enough to show the rule is false, so we must change the rule or stop using it. They also think that any other statement that looks like the case will also break the rule, so the rule has to be thrown out.

The thinking behind these ideas can be checked in a few ways. First, we have to be sure that the case really does match the kind of statement the rule talks about. If the case is not truly a statement that we know before we see any thing, or if it is not really a statement that is not just true by the meaning of its words, then the case does not hit the rule at all. Second, we have to see whether the part that says something about the thing really does hold inside the thing. If the writer is using a different sense of “hold inside” than the rule does, the clash may be only a matter of words, not of truth. Third, even if the case does break the rule, we must ask whether one case is enough to say the whole rule is wrong. Some rules are meant to work for most situations but allow rare exceptions. If the rule was meant to be absolute, then a single breaking case does show a problem; if the rule was meant to be a guide with room for odd cases, then the writer’s jump to “the rule must be thrown out” is too strong. In short, the writer’s first idea may be true if the case truly matches the rule, but the step from that idea to the claim that the rule is false is only good if the rule was meant to have no exceptions and if the case really does fit the rule in every needed way. If either of those points fails, the reasoning is not fully sound.

english_query: when we have good reason to think a thing will or will not happen
ugf_response: First, we look at the things we can see, hear, or feel that point to what might happen. When many of those things point the same way, we feel that the thing is likely to happen and we can say we have a good reason to think it will happen. When the things we see point the opposite way, we feel that the thing is not likely to happen and we have a good reason to think it will not happen. We also use what has happened in a time before that was like this one; if the same signs were there then and the result was a certain way, that gives us another good reason to think the same will happen or not happen now. So a good reason is just a lot of signs and past times that line up in a way that makes us feel sure about what will or will not happen.

english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed of atoms al
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that there is a case where a thing called S is not a basic kind of thing even though it is made of tiny parts that are all the same. In other words, they point to one example that does not fit the rule that being made of only one kind of tiny part is the same as being a basic kind of thing. If that example really exists, then the statement that every thing made of only one kind of tiny part must be a basic kind of thing is not always right.

The second idea that follows from that first idea is that because this one case does not fit, the whole rule cannot be true. They think that one bad case is enough to show that the rule does not work for all things, so the rule must be wrong.

The third idea is about whether that thinking is good. If the example truly does not fit the rule, then the step from the first idea to the second idea is a good step, because one counter case is enough to break a rule that says “all” things must follow it. But if the example actually does follow the rule when the words are understood the right way, then the step is not good, because they would be using a case that does not really go against the rule. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example really does break the rule or not.

english_query: abstraction analogy (argumentative) — allegory
ugf_response:
The first idea that the speaker says is true is that a simple picture or short story can be used to stand for a bigger idea that is not a thing you can touch. They point out that the picture or story has parts that look like parts of the bigger idea, and they say that this matching of parts shows that the picture or story is a good way to think about the bigger idea. This claim can be true when the picture or story really does share the same important parts as the bigger idea, because sharing parts means they are alike in a useful way.

The second idea that the speaker says follows from the first is that because the picture or story matches the bigger idea in some parts, we can take what the picture or story tells us and apply it to the bigger idea as a whole. They think that the match lets us use the picture or story to decide what is right or wrong about the bigger idea, or to predict how the bigger idea will act in other cases. In other words, they think the match lets us move from a small example to a big claim about the whole thing.

The thinking that links the first idea to the second idea is not always a good step. While a match of parts can help us see a similarity, it does not guarantee that the picture or story includes all the important parts of the bigger idea. If the picture or story leaves out something important, then using it to decide about the whole can lead us to a wrong belief. So the move from the first idea to the second idea works only when the picture or story truly covers every key part, and it can be a weak move when it does not. In short, the claim that the picture or story is true in its match may be true, but the claim that everything about the bigger idea follows from that match is only sometimes good and often needs more checking.

english_query: what it means to really know a thing and not just believe it
ugf_response: The first idea says that to really know a thing you need more than just to think it is true. In everyday life we can think something is true and still be wrong, so the claim that a simple thought is not enough to call something truly known matches what we see. The second idea says that if we only think something is true, then we should look for some sign, some way to see it work, or some way other people have shown it works before we say we really know it. This follows from the first idea because if a thought alone is not enough, we need something extra that can convince us that the thing is real. The third idea says that using that extra step helps us avoid being wrong and helps us make better choices, so the thinking behind it is a good one. It works well for many cases, though sometimes we do trust a word from someone we think is true without seeing it ourselves, so the line of thought is strong but not the only way we ever come to know a thing.

english_query: indicative conditional, constructive_dilemma — statutory
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is true is that there is a rule in the law that says if one thing happens then another thing will happen. The second idea that the person says is true is that there is another rule that says if a different thing happens then yet another thing will happen. The third idea that the person says is true is that either the first thing or the different thing must happen, so one of the two results must happen.

From those three ideas the person thinks that we can be sure that one of the two results will happen, and that the law therefore tells us what must be done in the case we are looking at. In other words, the person thinks that because the two rules are true and because one of the two starting things must be true, the law forces us to follow one of the two results.

To see if the thinking is good we need to check each idea. If the two rules in the law really say what the person says they do, and if it is really true that one of the two starting things must happen, then the step that says one of the two results must happen does follow and the thinking is good. If either of the two rules does not match the wording of the law, or if it is not true that one of the two starting things must happen, then the step that says one of the two results must happen does not follow and the thinking is not good. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the first, second, and third ideas are all correct and match the law. If they do, the reasoning makes sense; if any of them does not, the reasoning does not hold.

english_query: why counting up good and bad results is harder than it sounds
ugf_response: First, we see that to count good and bad things that happen we must first decide what counts as good and what counts as bad. Then we notice that people may see the same thing in different ways, so one person may call it good while another calls it bad and this means that the list we make can change depending on who is doing the counting. Next, we think about how many things can happen at once and we may have to add up many numbers and adding many numbers can be not easy because we have to keep track of which numbers belong to good and which belong to bad. Then we see that some things that happen are not clear, they may be partly good and partly bad, so we have to decide how to split them and this can make the total count change a lot. After that, we notice that the order in which we add the numbers can change the way we think about the total, because we may first add a big good number and feel that the total is high, then add a big bad number and feel that it drops, and this can make us think the total is not steady. Finally, we understand that all of these steps make counting good and bad things more not easy than it first seems, because we have to decide what counts, we have to keep track of many numbers, and we have to take care of things that are not clear.

english_query: the idea that the right thing is usually between two wrong things
ugf_response:
Person 1: I hear people say that the right thing is often between two wrong things. What does that mean?  
Person 2: It means that when we look at choices, one choice may be better than the others, even if all of them have some bad side.  
Person 1: So there is no perfect choice?  
Person 2: Right, there is rarely a perfect choice. We have to pick the one that has the least bad.  
Person 1: How do we know which one is the least bad?  
Person 2: We think about what will happen if we pick each one, and we try to see which result will hurt the fewest or help the most.  
Person 1: Can you give an example?  
Person 2: Imagine you have to decide whether to stay home and not help, or go out and risk getting hurt, or go out and help but maybe cause hurt to others. The middle option may be the best even though it still has risk.  
Person 1: I see. So the middle way is often the right way?  
Person 2: Yes, often the middle way is the one that avoids the biggest bad.

english_query: counterexample to: A property is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formu
ugf_response:
The first idea the speaker puts forward is a claim about what it means for a kind of thing to be named by a sentence in a set of rules for talking about math. They say that a kind of thing can be named by such a sentence exactly when there is a sentence that does the naming. In other words, they are saying that the way we say a thing can be named is the same as saying that a sentence that names it can be found.

The second idea they draw from that claim is that, because the two ways are the same, we can test whether a kind of thing can be named simply by looking for a sentence that names it. They also think that if we ever meet a kind of thing that we feel we can name but we cannot find any sentence that does the naming, then the original claim must be wrong. So they treat the claim as a tool that lets us decide what can be named just by searching for a sentence.

The third idea is an assessment of whether that way of thinking works well. The first part, that the meaning of “can be named by a sentence” is the same as “there is a sentence that names it,” is true by definition, so the speaker’s starting claim is not false. However, the step that this definition gives us a reliable way to find all such things is weaker. It assumes that we have the ability to write any sentence we might need, and it does not take into account that the set of sentences we can actually write may be limited. Because of that limitation, the conclusion that we can always tell what can be named just by looking for a sentence is not fully justified. The reasoning is therefore only partly good: the definition part is correct, but the jump from definition to a practical test is not entirely sound.

english_query: the idea that the right thing to do is what leads to the most good
ugf_response:
Person 1: What do we mean when we say the right thing to do is the one that brings the most good?  
Person 2: It means we look at what will make the biggest amount of good for the most people.  
Person 1: How do we know what will bring the most good?  
Person 2: We try to think about the results of each choice and see which one helps more people or helps them more.  
Person 1: What if one choice helps a lot of people but hurts a few?  
Person 2: Then we have to weigh the hurt against the help and see if the total good is still bigger.  
Person 1: Does that mean we always pick the choice with the biggest total good, even if it feels hard?  
Person 2: Yes, if we follow that idea we pick the choice that ends up with the most good overall, even if it is not easy.  
Person 1: Can we be sure we are right about the amount of good?  
Person 2: We can never be 100% sure, but we try to use the best facts we have and think carefully.

english_query: anomaly analogy (explanatory) — metaphor
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is true is that there is a thing that does not fit with the rest of the things we see. They point to this odd thing and say that it really is there, that it really does not act like the other things. This part is just a claim about what we can see, and it can be checked by looking at the thing for ourselves.

The second idea is what they think follows from that claim. They take the odd thing and use it as a picture to compare to some other thing. They say that because the odd thing works in a certain way, the other thing must work in the same way, and that this helps us understand why the other thing is the way it is. In other words, they move from the fact that the odd thing exists to the belief that the other thing should act like it.

The third idea is an assessment of whether that move is good. The step from one odd thing to a rule about another thing can be shaky because one odd thing may not tell us how all things work. The picture they use may not match the other thing closely enough, so the belief that the other thing must act the same way may not be well supported. Because of that, the thinking in the second idea is not very strong, and it may lead us to a wrong belief if we do not check more things.

english_query: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the event
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is a claim that a time point is in the past exactly when it has already happened. In other words, the claim says that if something has taken place then we can call that time past, and if we call a time past then it must have taken place. The person who makes the case says that this claim is true for the way we normally think about time.

The second idea is an example that is meant to show that the claim does not always work. The example picks a time that seems to have happened in one sense but not in the way the claim needs, or a time that seems to be past even though it has not happened. By pointing to this example, the person thinks they have proved that the claim is not always true. They then say that because the example exists, the claim must be wrong.

The third idea is the step where the person moves from the example to the judgment that the original claim is false. They think that one example that does not fit is enough to knock down the whole claim. This thinking is only partly good. It is good to test a claim with examples, and if an example truly shows a mismatch then the claim needs to be changed. However, the thinking would be bad if the example does not really match the situation the claim is about, or if the claim was meant to apply only to ordinary everyday times and not to the special case the example uses. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example really breaks the claim in the way the claim was intended. If the example does fit, then the thinking is good and the claim should be revised. If the example does not fit, then the thinking is not good and the claim may still be right for the ordinary cases.

english_query: when helping the group costs you but helps everyone
ugf_response:
The first idea they say is that when you give help to the group you lose something for yourself. This can be true because you may have to give your time, your money, or your effort, and that means you have less of those things for your own use. The next idea they think follows is that because the help makes all the people in the group get something, the loss you feel is okay and you should still give the help. They are saying that the fact that everyone gets help means the thing you lose is worth it. 

Now we can look at whether that thinking makes sense. It can make sense if the thing you lose is small and the thing the group gets is big, because then the overall result is good for most people. It does not make sense if the thing you lose is big and the thing the group gets is only a little, because then you pay a lot for a small gain. So the thought that you should always help the group just because it helps everyone is not always right. It is a good thought in some situations, but it needs to be checked against how much you lose and how much the group gains before we can say it is a good way to act.

english_query: strict conditional, paradoxical — inductive
ugf_response: The first idea says that there is a rule that says if one thing happens then another thing must happen, and that rule has no exceptions. The person also says that this rule makes a situation that looks like it is both true and not true at the same time, so it feels like a strange puzzle. The second idea is that because we have seen many times that when the first thing happens we also see the second thing, we can guess that the rule will work for every case we might meet. The person thinks that the many small things we have seen are enough to tell us that the rule is always right. The thinking about the first idea is partly right, because the rule can be true in some cases, but the claim that it never makes a strange puzzle is not right. The step from the many small things we have seen to the claim that the rule works for all cases is not a good step when the rule itself seems to give a puzzle that cannot be both true and not true. So the first part may be true, the second part does not follow safely, and the overall thinking is not a good way to be sure that the rule works in every case.

english_query: why some deals hold together and others fall apart
ugf_response: First, we see that two people make a promise to each other. Then we notice that each person wants something from the promise and that they both think they will get what they need if they keep it. This means that when both feel that the promise is fair and that they can trust each other, they will try to keep it. Next, we see that if one person starts to feel that the other is not fair or that the other is hurting them, they may stop keeping the promise. This shows that trust and fairness help the promise stay. Then we think about other things that can push a person, such as a need for money or a change in life, which can make it hard to keep the promise. This means that promises that are clear, feel fair, have strong trust, and are not under a big push are more likely to stay together. Finally, we see that when a promise is clear, both people feel it is fair, they have help from other people, and there is not a big push against it, the promise will stay, while a promise that is not clear, not fair, or under a big push is more likely to break and fall apart.

english_query: how to build your own set of rules for making hard choices
ugf_response: First, we think about what matters most to us when we have a hard choice. Then we write down the things we want to keep safe or help grow and use that list as a base to look at each choice. Next, we try to picture how each way we could go will change the things we care about. Then we see which way keeps the most of those things safe or helps them grow. After that we look to see if the way we choose fits with how we often act and with the kind of person we want to be. If it does, we go on and do it. If it does not, we go back and change the list of things we care about or think of another way. By repeating these steps each time we face a hard choice, we make our own guide that helps us choose in a way that feels right for us.

english_query: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent t
ugf_response: The first idea says that a thought that a person holds as true belongs to a certain kind exactly when the person is ready to say yes to that thing. This part is a statement about what it means for a thought to be of that kind. The second idea takes that statement and says that because it is true, we can decide whether any thought is of that kind simply by looking at whether the person is ready to say yes to it. The third idea brings in a case that tries to show the rule does not work. In this case a person has a thought that they are ready to say yes to a certain thing, but they never actually say yes because they never get a chance to hear the thing, or they have the thought but are not ready to say yes because they are not sure they will ever need to. This shows a mismatch between having the thought and being ready to say yes, so the rule that the two always go together is broken. The thinking in the third idea is good if the case really shows the mismatch. It does point out that the rule can miss situations where the person’s readiness and the thought do not line up, so the reasoning that the rule is not always right is strong. At the same time we have to check that the case uses the same meaning of “ready to say yes” that the rule talks about; if it does, the reasoning is solid, if it does not, the argument would be weaker. Overall the argument moves from a clear statement, to a claim about what follows, to a case that challenges the claim, and the challenge looks like a good reason to doubt the original rule.

english_query: counterexample to: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i
ugf_response:
The first idea says that the thing of giving a word to a thing works only when a certain state is true. In other words, the act of giving a word works to put that word on a thing exactly when the state that the first idea talks about is there. The person who makes the case says that this first idea is something we can see happen in some situations.

The second idea is that we can find a case where someone gives a word to a thing even though the state that the first idea says must be there is not there. Because we can see this case, the person thinks that the first idea cannot be right for every situation. They think that this case shows that the rule in the first idea does not hold in all cases.

The thinking in the second idea is good if the case really does lack the state that the first idea says is needed. If the case truly shows a word being put on a thing while the needed state is missing, then the conclusion that the first idea is not always true follows well. If, however, the case still has the needed state in a way that was not noticed, then the jump from the case to the conclusion would be weak. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example really breaks the condition that the first idea requires.

english_query: strict conditional, affirming_consequent — perceptual
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person puts forward is a rule that says if one thing happens, then another thing will happen. In other words, they say that when the first part is true, the second part must also be true. This is the fact that they claim to be true.  

The second idea that they take from this rule is that when they see the second part happen, they think the first part must have happened. They look at the result and then say that because the result is there, the cause that the rule talks about must also be there. This is the step they think follows from the first idea.  

When we look at this step, we see that it is not a good step. The rule only tells us that the first part leads to the second part, not that the second part can only come from the first part. The second part can happen for other reasons, so seeing the second part does not prove the first part. Because of that, the thinking that moves from the first idea to the second idea is a mistake. It is a bad way to move from what is said to what is thought, even though the first idea itself may be true.

english_query: how to think about what is fair when people start in different places
ugf_response: First, we notice that people do not all begin with the same amount of things like food, money, or chance. Then we think about what it means to be good or right when some have more and some have less. This can lead us to ask if we should give some help to the ones who have less so that they can have a chance to do the same things as the ones who have more. Next, we can look at what each person needs to be able to move forward and see if giving a little more to the ones who start with less will make the whole group work better. This means that a fair idea can be to try to make the start points more even, not by taking everything away from the ones who have more, but by adding a bit to the ones who have less. Finally, we see that thinking about fair in this way helps us decide how to share help, give chances, and make rules that try to let everyone have a fair chance to grow.

english_query: indicative conditional, denying_antecedent — child_rearing
ugf_response:
The first idea is a statement that says if a person who looks after a kid does a certain thing, then the kid will end up in a certain way. For example, it might say that if a mom reads to a kid every night, then the kid will learn to read well. That kind of statement can be true in some cases, because doing the first thing can often help bring about the second thing, but it is not a rule that works all the time for every kid and every family.

The second idea is what the speaker thinks must happen because the first part of the statement is not true. They say that because the mom does not read to the kid every night, the kid will not learn to read well. In other words, they take the “if … then …” sentence and turn it around, saying that if the first part is missing, the second part must also be missing.

The thinking behind that move is not a good one. Just because the first part does not happen does not mean the second part cannot happen in another way. A kid can still learn to read well from a teacher, a sibling, a book, or many other ways that do not need the mom to read every night. So the step from the first idea to the second idea is a mistake, even though the first idea itself might sometimes be true. The mistake makes the whole claim weak, because it assumes a one‑way link that does not have to hold in real life.

english_query: the problem with thinking only two choices exist when there are more
ugf_response: First, we notice that when we look at a thing we often think there are just two ways to do it. Then we see that this idea can stop us from looking at other ways that might be there. This means we may miss a better way that could help us more. Next, we think that when we only see two ways we may pick one that is not the best and then we may end up in a bad place. Then we realize that there are often many ways, and if we open our mind we can find a way that fits the need better. Finally, we understand that thinking there are only two ways can stop us and that looking for more ways can give us better results.

english_query: the idea that we are all connected and need each other
ugf_response:
Person 1: I hear people say we all must help each other. What does that mean?  
Person 2: It means that what one person does can change how another person lives, and we all share the same world.  
Person 1: How can one person change another if they never meet?  
Person 2: Even if they do not meet, the first person can change the air, the water, the food, or the ideas that move, and those things reach the other person.  
Person 1: So we have to look after each other to stay safe?  
Person 2: Yes, we have to look after each other because when one is hurt, the whole group can feel the hurt too.  
Person 1: Does that mean we should share what we have?  
Person 2: Sharing makes the group stronger, because when one has more and gives some away, everyone can have enough.  
Person 1: I think I understand now. We are like a big family that lives together in this world.

english_query: literal_similarity analogy (heuristic) — philosophy_of_mind
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is that when two things have the same looking part, we can say that they have the same inner part too. In some cases this can be true, for example when two objects are made in the same way, the same looking part can mean the same inner part. In other cases it is not true, because two things can look the same on the outside but work in very different ways inside. So the claim that the same looking part always means the same inner part is only partly true.

The second idea they take from that first claim is that, because the two things share a same looking part, we can use a short way to think about how the way we think and feel works. In other words they think that if a small part of a brain looks like a small part of a computer, then the whole brain must work like a whole computer. This jump from the first claim to the whole way we think and feel is a big step. It follows only if the first claim were always true, and if the small part really shows how the whole works. Since the first claim is not always true, the jump does not hold in many cases.

The thinking behind the case can be checked for how good it is. The use of a compare of same looking parts can be a useful short way to think when we do not have more information, but it can also lead us to think that the whole is the same when it is not. The reasoning moves from a small observation to a big statement without enough proof, so the reasoning is weak. A better way would be to first check many examples to see when the same looking part really means the same inner part, and only then try to say something about the whole way we think and feel. In short, the first idea is only sometimes true, the second idea follows only if the first idea were always true, and the thinking does not give a strong support for the big statement.

english_query: how to build fair rules if you did not know your place in the world
ugf_response: First, we think about why we need rules that act the same for all people even if we do not know where we will end up. Then we see that we can start by making a rule that looks at each person just as a person, not at where they are or what they have. This means we do not need to know our own spot when we set the rule. Next, we can add that the rule should work the same if any person later finds themselves in a high spot or a low spot. This shows that the rule must not use any idea about a person’s spot. Then we think about a way to try the rule: we can imagine two different spots, one with a lot and one with little, and see if the rule would make us act the same way in both. If it does, the rule is fair. Finally, we understand that by making the rule only about what each person does and not about where they are, we can set a rule that stays fair even when we do not know our own spot.

english_query: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal mem
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is that there is a case where an action is shown by every usual person in a group, yet we still do not think of that action as something we do without learning. They say that this case shows the rule “an action is something we do without learning only when it is shown by every usual person” is not true. In other words, they claim that the fact they can point to a real example where the rule does not match what we think proves the rule is wrong.

The second idea they draw from that fact is that because the rule does not work for this one case, the rule itself must be changed. They think we need to add more parts to the rule or replace it with a different way of telling when an action is something we do without learning. In their view the single case is enough to make us give up the old rule and look for a better description.

The thinking behind the argument can be looked at in three ways. First, the case they give does seem to be a real situation, so the claim that the example exists is true. Second, the step from “the rule does not match this one case” to “the whole rule must be wrong” is a strong step. It works if the rule says it must hold for every case, but many rules are meant to work only for most cases or only when other hidden ideas are also true. Third, the conclusion that we need a new rule is reasonable only if we are sure there is no other way to explain why the example looks different. If we can show that the example is actually a special situation, or that the rule was meant to include extra ideas that were not mentioned, then the jump to a new rule is not fully justified. So while the first idea is correct and the second idea follows in a simple way, the overall reasoning may be too quick because it does not check whether the rule might still be useful with a small change or with an added condition.

english_query: counterexample to: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if
ugf_response: The first idea they put forward is that a story about a thing that happened in the past can be a good enough story to tell why it happened only if the story meets certain simple checks. They then show a case that seems to meet those checks but still does not work as a good enough story for the thing, and they say this shows that the original claim about what makes a good enough story is not always true. The second idea they draw from that case is that because one example can break the original claim, the original claim must be wrong in general. In other words, they think that the failure of the claim in this single case means the claim cannot be trusted for any case. To see whether that thinking is good we have to ask if the single case really does break the claim in the way they say. If the case truly follows the same simple checks as the claim requires and still fails to give a good enough story, then the step from the case to the claim being wrong is a strong step and the thinking is good. However, if the case does not actually follow all the checks, or if the claim was meant to allow extra things that the case does not have, then the step is weak and the thinking is not good. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the case matches the checks and whether the jump from one broken case to the whole claim being false is justified.

english_query: why following rules is not enough if you do not care about people
ugf_response: The first idea says that a rule can be read, learned and then done just by looking at the words on the paper, and that a person can do the rule without thinking about any other person at all. The second idea says that because the rule was done, the job is finished and nothing else needs to be added, even if the people who are around might feel hurt or left out. The third idea says that if a person does not think about the people they affect, the rule may still leave a gap where something important is missed, and that gap can make the result not as good as it could be. This thinking is not all right because it forgets that the goal of many rules is to keep people safe and happy, and a rule by itself does not always catch every situation that can happen. So while the first idea is true that a rule can be followed without caring, the second idea that this is enough is not a good thought, because caring about the people around us helps fill the places where the rule does not reach and makes the whole result better.

english_query: what makes someone happy in the deepest way, not just for a moment
ugf_response:
Person 1: What do you think makes a person feel very happy, not just for a short time?  
Person 2: I think it is when a person feels that they belong and can give something to others.  
Person 1: Why does belonging help more than a quick fun thing?  
Person 2: Because belonging makes a person feel safe and part of a group, and that feeling stays even when the fun ends.  
Person 1: Can you give an example of something that can give that feeling?  
Person 2: A friendship where you share and care for each other can give that feeling.  
Person 1: How does giving to others add to that deep happy feeling?  
Person 2: When you give, you see that you can help, and that makes you feel that you matter and that your life has a use.

english_query: analogy_proper analogy (predictive) — physics
ugf_response:
The first idea is that the way the person puts two things side by side is a right way and that this side by side can be used to tell what will happen later in the study of how things move and work. This claim can be true when the two things share the same important parts and act in the same simple way. The first idea is not always true, because if the two things are only alike in a small way, the side by side may not help us tell what will happen later.

The second idea is that because the side by side works in one case, it will also work in the other case, so we can trust the guess about what will happen later. This step follows from the first idea if we accept that the two things are alike in the ways that matter. The second idea says that the guess we make from the first case can be used as a guide for the second case.

The third idea is whether this line of thinking is good. It is good when the two things really do share the same important parts and do not have big differences that change how they act. It is not good when the things only look alike on the outside but have hidden parts that work differently, because then the guess can be weak and lead us away from the right answer. So the thinking can be strong if the side by side is true in the important ways, and it can be weak if the side by side hides important differences.
